newHtml:
A pit was dug to hold the condemned for stoning — institutional preparation Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Moderate Abu Dawud 4442
"He ordered that a pit be dug for her, and he ordered that she be stoned."

[Commentary:] "It is allowed to dig a pit for stoning to death as the punishment for illegal..."

What the hadith says

Stoning executions were preceded by preparation: a pit was dug to hold the condemned in place. Abu Dawud's collection commentary normalizes this as permitted practice.

Why this is a problem

The infrastructure of the pit demonstrates deliberateness. Stoning is not presented as an emotional communal response but as a scheduled, prepared execution. The pit's function is to prevent escape while stones are thrown by multiple people over a period ranging from minutes to hours. This is the engineering of suffering, not its incidental occurrence.

The tradition's own commentary confirms the legalization: "it is allowed to dig a pit." Modern implementations have followed this specification — Iran's penal code until recently included detailed pit-depth and stone-size requirements, continuous with the jurisprudential tradition Abu Dawud preserves.

Why it fails

"Reduces suffering" concedes the logic of calibrated execution while defending its design. The pit's function is to hold the victim immobile while others throw stones; it does not shorten death. The rarity argument is historically selective — stonings have occurred across Islamic history and continue in the modern era. The institutional apparatus is the problem, independent of deployment frequency.

Ali burned apostates alive — Ibn Abbas cited a prophetic prohibition on fire-punishment Prophetic Character Contradiction Moderate Abu Dawud 4351
"I would not have burned them with fire, because the Messenger of Allah said: 'Do not punish with the punishment of Allah.' I would have executed them in accordance with the words of the Messenger of Allah, because the Messenger of Allah said: 'Whoever changes his religion, execute him.'"

What the hadith says

Ibn Abbas objected to Ali's burning of certain apostates: fire is Allah's prerogative, not a human punishment. He should have executed them by sword instead, per the prophetic rule that apostasy is a capital offense.

Why this is a problem

The dispute is entirely about method: both Ibn Abbas and Ali agree that apostates should die. Ibn Abbas's moral instinct — fire is wrong — is preserved in the canonical record. The underlying conviction — execution is right — is not questioned by either party. The tradition archived a debate about technique while leaving the core question (should apostates be killed?) entirely outside the scope of moral inquiry.

Ali's burning of human beings alive for apostasy is preserved as a historical fact, documented by the fourth caliph and first imam of Shia Islam without causing any tradition to question his fitness for that role. The event is treated as a jurisprudential case study, not as a moral scandal.

Why it fails

The moral critique preserved is about the specific instrument of execution, not about the execution itself. A tradition whose most prominent internal correction is "burn less, behead more" has not demonstrated moral reasoning — it has demonstrated procedural refinement within a framework it never interrogates. The question of whether apostasy-killing is right is the question the tradition has always refused to ask.

"They are from them" — night raids permitting incidental killing of women and children Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Moderate Abu Dawud 2666
"[The Prophet was asked] whether it was permissible to attack the pagan warriors at night with the probability of exposing their women and children to danger. The Prophet replied: 'They (women and children) are from them (pagans).'"

What the hadith says

When asked whether to proceed with a night raid knowing women and children would probably be killed alongside fighters, Muhammad's answer was: they are from the enemy. The raid is permitted.

Why this is a problem

The question was specifically about foreseeable non-combatant deaths. The answer was not "minimize harm" or "avoid killing the innocent" — it was a categorical statement that enemy women and children share the enemy's legal status. This is the original "collective guilt" ruling, which eliminates the civilian-combatant distinction as a limiting principle in the specific context of night raids.

Other hadiths do forbid the deliberate targeting of women and children, which classical jurists used to construct a distinction between deliberate killing (forbidden) and incidental killing (permitted). This is functionally identical to the modern "collateral damage" doctrine — invented in medieval Islamic jurisprudence on the basis of texts like this one, centuries before the Geneva Conventions.

Why it fails

The edge case matters enormously when it has been cited by modern jihadi groups to justify attacks that kill women and children. "They are from them" is the textual anchor for arguments that civilian affiliation with the enemy makes them legitimate targets — and that reading follows from the hadith's own grammar. A text used to authorize civilian casualties in modern contexts is not a mere historical edge case.

Aisha played with dolls while married to the Prophet Women Prophetic Character Strange / Obscure Moderate Abu Dawud 4932
"'Aishah's dolls that she played with..."

What the hadith says

The hadith corpus preserves that Aisha continued playing with dolls during her marriage to Muhammad. He saw her and smiled. Her girl-companions came over to play with her.

Why this is a problem

The dolls are biographical evidence about Aisha's developmental stage. A girl who is sexually active with her husband but still plays with toys has not reached developmental adulthood by any standard outside of narrow physiological readiness. The tradition preserves both the consummation age and the doll-play — two data points that cannot be reconciled without conceding that the tradition's concept of marital readiness was limited to physical puberty, not developmental wholeness.

The apologetic that cites Muhammad's tolerance of the play as evidence of his gentleness concedes the very premise it is trying to dispel: his wife was developmentally still a child, which is why he "let" her play with toys rather than treating her as an adult peer.

Why it fails

The non-isolation defense concedes the developmental profile while reframing it positively. Defenders who argue Aisha was older at consummation cannot consistently accept the doll-playing as historical — the two positions require each other's rejection. Those who accept both must acknowledge that "sexually active" and "playing with dolls" described the same person at the same time — which is the problem, not its resolution.

Muhammad was forbidden to pray for his own mother's forgiveness Prophetic Character Contradiction Moderate Abu Dawud 3234
"I asked my Lord for permission to seek forgiveness for my mother, but He did not permit me. And I asked Him for permission to visit her grave, and He permitted me."

What the hadith says

Muhammad sought Allah's permission to pray for his mother Aminah's forgiveness — she died before his prophethood and was therefore a pre-Islamic pagan. Allah refused permission. Muhammad was allowed only to visit the grave.

Why this is a problem

Aminah's "fault" was being born in a time and place before Islam existed. She had no access to the religion her son would later found. On Islamic orthodox theology, she is among the disbelievers who cannot be forgiven — not because of any moral failure, but because of historical accident of birth. The Prophet of divine mercy cannot obtain mercy for his own mother.

Q 35:18 says no soul bears another's burden. Aminah's burden is that she lived before Islam — not a moral choice, but a temporal fact. A religion's treatment of those who preceded its founding is a test of its claim to universal mercy, and Islamic orthodoxy fails that test at the Prophet's own graveside.

Why it fails

The hadith is unambiguous: Allah refused permission to seek her forgiveness. Whatever the theoretical ahl al-fatra doctrine may allow, this specific hadith closes the question for Aminah specifically. The apologetic reaches for a general doctrine to override a specific refusal — and the specific refusal is what the tradition preserved.

Men who imitate women and women who imitate men — cursed by the Prophet Women Prophetic Character Moderate Abu Dawud 4097
"The Messenger of Allah cursed men who imitate women, and women who imitate men."

What the hadith says

Muhammad pronounced a divine curse (la'na) on gender-nonconforming presentation in both directions — men presenting as women, and women presenting as men.

Why this is a problem

The curse is pronounced for presentation choices — not for harm caused to another person. There is no victim of gender-nonconforming dress or manner. Yet the punishment is divine condemnation. This places people who experience their gender differently from their birth-assigned sex under permanent prophetic curse for the act of living as themselves.

The hadith's real-world trajectory is direct and documented: from medieval jurisprudence (Ibn Taymiyyah, al-Nawawi) treating mukhannathun as a restricted legal class, to contemporary anti-LGBTQ enforcement in Iran, Saudi Arabia, and Malaysia that cites this and parallel hadiths as prophetic precedent. A divine curse for presentation is not abstract theology — it is the foundation of state persecution.

Why it fails

The hadith's language is not restricted to deceptive intent — it covers any man who imitates women or woman who imitates men. Classical jurisprudence applied it generally to effeminate manner, speech, and dress. The "7th-century Arabia didn't know about gender dysphoria" observation is accurate but does not rescue the text: a curse on presentation that people cannot choose condemns people for their involuntary nature. That is not a limitation of the hadith's context; it is a description of its harm.

"The Stoning of the Two Jews" — a dedicated Abu Dawud chapter Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Moderate Abu Dawud Book 38 (Legal Punishments), Chapter 26
[Chapter title:] "The Stoning Of The Two Jews" — two Jews brought to Muhammad for adultery; he applied the Torah stoning penalty; they were executed.

What the hadith says

Muhammad adjudicated an adultery case involving two Jews, applied Torah law's stoning penalty, and executed them — extending Islamic judicial authority over a non-Muslim community with capital consequences.

Why this is a problem

Muhammad's claim to Torah expertise is internally contradictory. Islamic theology holds the Torah has been altered and is unreliable — yet here Muhammad appeals to the Torah as authoritative enough to execute people. A prophet cannot selectively invoke the authority of a text he otherwise dismisses as corrupted.

The narrative framing is also polemical: a rabbi covers the stoning verse with his hand; Muhammad exposes it. The villain is a Jew hiding scripture; the hero is the Arab prophet revealing concealment. This is a story constructed for an audience unfamiliar with how publicly available Torah scrolls functioned — its rhetorical structure requires the listener to accept that Jews hide their own texts, which is antisemitic premise, not documented history.

Why it fails

Enforcing another community's law on them while claiming their scripture is corrupted is not principled consistency — it is selective invocation. A prophet applying a death penalty from a text he elsewhere calls unreliable is using that text instrumentally, not respectfully. The execution of two Jews under Torah authority cited by an Islamic prophet who rejected Torah authority is a contradiction the apologetic cannot dissolve.

Adam wins the argument against Moses — his sin was pre-decreed, so he bears no blame Logical Inconsistency Contradiction Moderate Abu Dawud 4701
"Moses argued with Adam... Adam said: 'Moses, Allah chose you by His speech with you, and He wrote the Tawrah for you with His hand; yet you blame me for a matter that Allah had decreed for me forty years before He created me.' Thus Adam refuted Moses."

What the hadith says

Moses confronts Adam for his expulsion from Eden; Adam argues his sin was divinely decreed before his creation. Muhammad endorses Adam's argument as the winner.

Why this is a problem

If Adam cannot be blamed because his sin was predestined, every human sinner has the same defense. Islamic criminal law and its punishments — lashing, amputation, execution — all assume moral agency. If Adam's pre-destiny defense succeeds, any defendant could invoke it. The hadith validates a fatalism that renders punishment incoherent.

Islamic theology spent centuries debating free will precisely because hadiths like this one create the problem. The Qadariyya (free will defenders) and Jabariyya (hard determinists) both existed as Muslim theological schools because the corpus pulled in both directions. The unresolved tension is directly traceable to this kind of authoritative fatalist hadith.

Why it fails

The hadith says Adam "refuted" Moses — the word indicates a decisive win in the dispute, not a consolation offered. The Ash'arite kasb doctrine is an attempt to reconcile this tension, but kasb's coherence is disputed even within Islamic philosophy. A tradition whose most authoritative predestination statement has an endorsed winning argument for "my sin was decreed, so blame is inapplicable" has created the irresolvability it spent centuries trying to solve.

The one-eyed Dajjal — borrowed eschatology with borrowed costume Strange / Obscure Jesus / Christology Moderate Abu Dawud Book 37 (Trials), #4317-#4328
[Abu Dawud on the Dajjal:] one eye, "kafir" on his forehead, forty-day reign, defeat by Jesus at the Lydda gate.

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud preserves extensive hadiths on the Dajjal — one-eyed, forehead-marked, 40-day reign, killed by Jesus returning to earth.

Why this is a problem

The Dajjal figure is recognizably assembled from pre-Islamic sources: the one-eyed chaos monster is ancient Near Eastern iconography; the Antichrist figure who deceives the world before a messianic return is Jewish-Christian eschatology; Jesus returning to kill the Antichrist is a Christian plot device that Islam imported and reoriented. The specific detail of Jesus killing the Dajjal at the Lydda gate further ties the narrative to a Palestinian geography whose eschatological significance is Christian, not originally Islamic.

Throughout Islamic history, the detailed specifications have enabled repeated misidentification: dozens of claimants have presented themselves as the Mahdi or been accused of being the Dajjal on the basis of partial pattern-matching. A prophecy easily misapplied is a prophecy that generates harm regardless of its ultimate fulfillment.

Why it fails

The "all traditions converge on truth" defense cannot explain why the Islamic Dajjal narrative borrows so specifically from Jewish-Christian Antichrist traditions while differing from them in ways that align with Islamic theological interests (Jesus is Muslim, breaks crosses, kills swine). The modifications are doctrinally motivated, not independently received. That pattern is the fingerprint of a text engaging its literary environment, not transcending it.

The penalty for a Muslim magician: execution by sword Strange / Obscure Prophetic Character Moderate Abu Dawud 3043
"The legal punishment for the magician is a strike with the sword."

What the hadith says

Muhammad prescribed the death penalty for those practicing magic (sihr) — divination, sorcery, and similar occult practices.

Why this is a problem

"Magician" is a folk category, not a verifiable capability. Any accusation of sihr — folk healing, non-Muslim religious practice, personal vendetta — could trigger a capital charge with no standard of proof adequate to distinguish magic from other behavior. Saudi Arabia has executed people for "sorcery" as recently as 2012, using this hadith as legal anchor.

The rule also contradicts the Prophet's own biography: other hadiths preserve that Muhammad himself was successfully bewitched by a Jewish man named Labid (Bukhari). A prophet who was bewitched implies magic is real and powerful — which makes the magician genuinely dangerous and the death penalty less arbitrary, but also more deeply embeds magical thinking into the legal system.

Why it fails

Demonstrably destructive occult harm cannot be proven by any objective evidentiary standard, because magic has no verified causal mechanism. Any legal system that executes for a crime whose definition is "causing supernatural harm" is executing based on accusation and belief, not evidence of cause. The historical and contemporary pattern of sihr accusations confirms that the rule operates on suspicion, not proof.

A black dog is Satan — and a woman invalidates prayer like a dog or donkey Strange / Obscure Women Moderate Abu Dawud 702
"[The prayer is broken by] a donkey, or a black dog, or a woman (passing in front of him)... The black dog is a Shaitan."

What the hadith says

A man's prayer is invalidated if a donkey, a black dog, or a woman passes in front of him. Black dogs specifically are identified as Satan.

Why this is a problem

Woman is listed alongside two animals as a prayer-invalidating presence. Not a woman in specific circumstances — just a woman. The grammar equates her ritual status with that of a donkey and a specifically-identified demonic animal. Aisha explicitly objected to this teaching, saying "You have made us equivalent to dogs and donkeys" — and her objection is preserved in Bukhari. The tradition kept the ruling and her objection.

The color-coded demonology — black dogs are Satan, others are not — is folk magic with no theological foundation. A Creator who designed dogs does not discriminate their metaphysical status by coat color. The hadith preserves a cultural superstition as religious law.

Why it fails

Aisha's objection — which was not accepted as overriding the ruling — confirms rather than mitigates the problem: the Prophet's own wife identified the grammatical equating of women with animals and was preserved in the canon as having raised the objection, while the ruling stood. A tradition that kept both the rule and the protest without resolving the conflict has made the discomfort part of the record.

All musical instruments forbidden — except the daff hand drum Strange / Obscure Logical Inconsistency Moderate Abu Dawud Book 43 (Adab), Chapters 51-52
"[Singing and playing] wind instruments is disliked..." [Chapter heading] "Instruments other than the Daff are prohibited." [Commentary on #4922]

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud preserves the mainstream Sunni ruling: wind and string instruments are forbidden; only the daff (hand drum) is permitted. Listeners who enjoy forbidden music are warned they will be transformed into apes and pigs at the last day.

Why this is a problem

The daff exception is arbitrary: a drum is a musical instrument. The stated theological principle — music is Satan's tool — applies equally to percussion. The exception exists because the daff was used at the Prophet's own wedding celebrations, so it could not be banned; all other instruments were then prohibited around this grandfathered exception. The rule is not principled; it is customary with one item excepted.

Music is a universal human practice predating Islam by tens of thousands of years. A universal religion that categorizes a core human art form as satanic has positioned itself against human nature. The Taliban and various Islamic governments have implemented this ruling with cultural devastation as the result.

Why it fails

The classical disagreement is real but does not rescue the hadith's content. Abu Dawud's chapters exist, the chapter headings are clear, and the transformation-into-apes-and-pigs threat for music listeners is preserved as authoritative. A tradition whose sahih-grade texts threaten listeners with animal transformation for enjoying music and then claims internal diversity on the question has an unanswered problem at its canonical center.
